The system is built from governed components ג€” each component that uses LLMs must have a defined role created through a multi-LLM review board.\n\nDuring the first attempt to create the agent-role-builder's own role (the bootstrap proof that the tool works), we discovered that the review process itself had fundamental gaps. Across 6 runs and 20+ review rounds, the board couldn't converge ג€” reviewers kept finding new issues at the same rate old ones were fixed.\n\nRoot cause analysis revealed five problems:\n1. Reviewer feedback was flat text lists with no severity or structure ג€” the implementer couldn't tell what was critical vs cosmetic\n2. The implementer received raw JSON blobs instead of actionable fix instructions ג€” it had to re-parse and interpret feedback each round\n3. No institutional memory existed ג€” the same types of issues kept appearing because there was no accumulation of \"don't do this\" rules\n4. Both reviewers ran every round even when one already approved ג€” wasting time and creating noise\n5. No defined process for what happens when reviewers disagree or when the review budget runs out\n\nThis architecture solves all five problems and creates a self-improving system that gets better with every review cycle it runs.\n\n---\n\n## Core Principle: Everything Is Boxed\n\nEvery component in ADF is self-contained in its own directory. All files related to that component live inside its directory ג€” not scattered across the project.\n\nA component may contain multiple LLM agents. Each LLM agent gets its own subfolder with its own rulebook and review prompt. For example, the COO component has controller/, classifier/, intelligence/, and context-engineer/ ג€” each LLM-powered layer (classifier, intelligence) has its own subfolder with its own governance artifacts.\n\nThis means any agent can understand a component by reading its directory. No hunting across the project for related files.\n\nPer-component (or per-LLM-agent subfolder):\n- **Source code** ג€” the implementation\n- **Rulebook** (`rulebook.json`) ג€” accumulated rules from past review failures\n- **Review prompt** (`review-prompt.json`) ג€” defines what reviewers should focus on for this component\n- **Run artifacts** ג€” permanent evidence of every review cycle\n- **Role definition** ג€” if the component uses LLMs, its governed role lives here too\n\n---\n\n## The Rulebook\n\nEvery component that goes through governed review must have a `rulebook.json` in its own directory. This is the component's institutional memory.\n\n**What rules are:**\nRules are high-level, generalized core essence directives distilled from real review failures. They capture the principle behind the failure, not the specific incident. Examples:\n- \"Define authority precedence explicitly when multiple governance layers exist\"\n- \"Never reference the same concept with different phrasings across sections without a canonical definition\"\n- \"Completion criteria must be testable against every terminal state, not just the happy path\"\n- \"Always do null checks on scope resolution results\"\n- \"Don't swallow errors ג€” surface them with source and context\"\n\n**What rules are NOT:**\n- Not copies of reviewer feedback (\"round 2 said authority chain is ambiguous\")\n- Not implementation details (\"add a null check on line 47\")\n- Not temporary fixes (\"workaround for the encoding bug\")\n\n**Rule structure (JSON):**\nEach rule has an ID, the generalized principle, the source review cycle that produced it, and a version. Rules can be superseded but never silently deleted ג€” history is preserved in git.\n\n**Who creates rules:**\nThe learning engine (described below) extracts rules from review feedback. The implementer doesn't write rules ג€” they follow them.\n\n---\n\n## The Learning Engine\n\nA shared generic service that lives in `shared/learning-engine/`. Its job is to analyze review feedback and extract generalizable rules that prevent the same failures from recurring.\n\n**How it works:**\nAfter each review round and before any fix attempt, the learning engine receives the reviewer feedback and asks: \"What generalizable principle does this failure reveal?\" It produces new rulebook entries (or identifies that existing rules already cover the issue). It updates the specific component's `rulebook.json` ג€” the file in that component's directory, not a generic global file.\n\n**Why it's generic:**\nThe extraction logic is the same regardless of what's being reviewed. \"What pattern does this failure reveal?\" works for role definitions, TypeScript code, system prompts, and architecture documents. The only thing that changes is the prompt context.\n\n**How prompts are used:**\nEach component holds a `review-prompt.json` in its own directory. This prompt tells the learning engine what domain it's operating in (design review, code review, prompt review, architecture review) and what kinds of rules are relevant. The learning engine loads this prompt to contextualize its analysis. Without it, the engine doesn't know what domain patterns to look for.\n\n**Why it runs between review and fix:**\nIf the implementer fixes issues without first extracting the rule, the same class of issue will appear in future runs. The learning step ensures the rulebook grows before the fix is attempted. The implementer then reads the updated rulebook before fixing, which means the fix addresses the root pattern, not just the specific symptom.\n\n---\n\n## The Review Process (Per Round)\n\nEach review round follows this sequence:\n\n### Step 1: Implementer produces or revises artifacts\nThe implementer (an LLM) creates or revises the component artifact (role definition, code, prompt, etc.). On the first round, this is generated from the request. On subsequent rounds, it's revised based on prior feedback.\n\n### Step 2: Implementer produces delta compliance map\nThe implementer checks its work against the rulebook and produces a JSON compliance map showing:\n- Which rules were checked\n- Where in the artifact each rule is satisfied\n- Only for sections that changed (delta) ג€” not the entire artifact on middle rounds\n- References the git commit or artifact version being checked\n\nThis gives the reviewer a head start ג€” they can verify the implementer's claims instead of rediscovering known issues from scratch.\n\n**Full sweep rule:**\n- First round: full compliance map (all rules against entire artifact)\n- Middle rounds: delta compliance map (only changed sections)\n- Final round (when reviewer finds no pushbacks): full sweep to catch cross-cutting issues\n- This balances thoroughness with efficiency\n\n### Step 3: Implementer produces fix items map (rounds 2+)\nOn subsequent rounds (after a review rejection), the implementer produces a fix items map showing for each reviewer finding:\n- `accepted`: what was changed, where, and a summary of the fix\n- `rejected`: why the finding is invalid (missing context, contradicts another requirement, etc.)\n\nThis serves as both proof of fix AND reviewer context for the next round. The reviewer doesn't have to guess what changed ג€” the map tells them.\n\n### Step 4: Review\nReviewers (Codex + Claude pair minimum) independently review the artifact. Their feedback uses a structured contract (JSON):\n- **Verdict**: `approved` (ready to freeze), `conditional` (minor fixes only), or `reject` (blocking/major issues)\n- **Conceptual groups**: findings grouped by root cause, not flat lists. Each group has:\n  - Severity: `blocking`, `major`, `minor`, or `suggestion`\n  - Specific findings with source section references\n  - Redesign guidance: what to do about it\n- **Residual risks**: things that aren't blocking but need tracking\n- **Strengths**: what's good (not just criticism)\n- **Fix items decision map** (rounds 2+): for each item in the implementer's fix map:\n  - `accept_fix`: the fix resolves the finding\n  - `reject_fix`: the fix is insufficient (with reason why)\n  - `accept_rejection`: the implementer was right to reject the finding\n  - `reject_rejection`: the implementer's rejection is invalid (with reason why)\n\nThis creates a proper negotiation protocol between implementer and reviewer, not a one-way broadcast. The implementer has freedom to push back on findings that lack context or contradict other requirements, and the reviewer must explicitly respond to each pushback.\n\n### Step 5: On review failure (reject verdict)\nWhen a reviewer rejects:\n1. Learning engine digests the feedback and updates the component's `rulebook.json`\n2. A fresh implementer receives: current artifact + updated rulebook + actionable fix checklist + prior compliance map\n3. The implementer first mechanically walks every rule in the rulebook against the artifact, then addresses the specific feedback items\n4. The implementer produces both a new compliance map (delta) and a fix items map\n5. The round proceeds to the next review cycle\n\n### Step 6: On review pass (approved/conditional)\nWhen all reviewers approve or go conditional:\n- Conditional items are fixed (specific minor changes only)\n- Leader confirms freeze\n- Artifacts promoted to canonical location\n- Decision log and board summary written\n\n---\n\n## Split Verdict Handling\n\nWhen one reviewer approves and another rejects:\n\n1. Fix only the rejecting reviewer's findings\n2. Re-run only the rejecting reviewer in the next round ג€” don't waste the approving reviewer's time\n3. When the rejecting reviewer approves or goes conditional, run one final sanity check with the previously-approving reviewer to catch any regressions the fixes may have introduced\n4. Both must approve/conditional before the artifact can freeze\n\nThis cuts review cycles significantly ג€” prior runs re-ran both reviewers every round even when one was satisfied.\n\n---\n\n## Arbitration (Minor Items Only)\n\nArbitration is strictly limited to prevent swallowing real issues:\n\n**When arbitration is allowed:**\n- Only on `minor` and `suggestion` severity items where reviewers disagree on whether something needs fixing\n- The leader can decide \"this is acceptable as-is\" for these items only\n- The decision is logged with full rationale\n\n**When arbitration is NOT allowed:**\n- Never on `blocking` or `major` severity items\n- Never to override a `reject` verdict\n- Never to force a freeze when material pushback remains\n\n**Budget exhaustion protocol (final round):**\nWhen the review budget is about to run out and blocking/major items remain:\n- The artifact does NOT freeze. Blocking issues cannot be swallowed.\n- Each reviewer picks their single most important remaining fix (ultimatum)\n- The implementer fixes those specific items only\n- The decision is logged with full audit trail: what was fixed, what was deferred, and why\n- The run exits as `resume_required` with deferred items documented\n- The cycle post-mortem (described below) analyzes why the budget wasn't sufficient\n\n**Arbitration result:**\nWhen arbitration is used, the result must reflect it as partial acceptance ג€” `frozen_with_conditions` (distinct from clean `frozen`). The result lists all deferred minor items. The invoking component (the caller that requested the role/review) can then:\n- Accept the conditional result\n- Request more rounds to achieve full acceptance\n\nThe invoker has authority, not the tool.\n\n---\n\n## All Contracts Are JSON\n\nEvery input and output in this architecture is strict JSON with defined schemas. No markdown for machine interfaces. Markdown is for human-readable documentation only.\n\nThis includes:\n- Rulebook entries\n- Compliance maps\n- Fix items maps\n- Review results\n- Learning engine outputs\n- Post-mortem reports\n- Run artifacts\n\nWhy: JSON is parseable, validatable, and unambiguous. Markdown is prose ג€” it can't be mechanically verified. When the compliance map says \"rule R-003 satisfied at <authority> line 12\", that's a verifiable claim. When a markdown file says \"authority is defined\", that's an opinion.\n\n---\n\n## Permanent Audit Trail\n\nAll run artifacts are committed to git. Nothing is volatile. The structure:\n\n```\n<component>/\n  rulebook.json\n  review-prompt.json\n  runs/\n    <job-id>/\n      rounds/\n        round-0/\n          review.json          # reviewer verdicts (structured)\n          learning.json        # learning engine output (new rules)\n          compliance-map.json  # implementer's delta compliance check\n          fix-items-map.json   # implementer's fix/reject map (round 1+)\n          diff-summary.json    # what changed from prior round\n        round-1/\n          ...\n      result.json              # terminal result\n      run-postmortem.json      # tier 1 KPI\n      cycle-postmortem.json    # tier 2 summary (when job completes)\n```\n\n**Commit timing:**\n- **Commit**: at the end of every round (all round artifacts). Preserves evidence atomically per round.\n- **Push**: at the end of every cycle (when job reaches terminal state). Rounds accumulate locally, push happens once when done.\n- Exception: if a round takes unusually long (watchdog timeout), commit before the next round starts so evidence isn't lost if the process crashes.\n\n---\n\n## Error Escalation Pattern (System-Wide)\n\nWhen any governed component hits an unrecoverable error during execution:\n\n### Step 1: Stop Immediately\nDo not retry, do not parse-hack, do not silently continue. The component stops at the point of failure.\n\n### Step 2: Produce Structured Bug Report\nThe bug report (`bug-report.json` in the run directory) must include:\n- **What failed**: error type, error message, stack trace if available\n- **Where**: which step/phase the component was in (e.g., \"Round 2, leader response parsing\")\n- **Context**: current execution state ג€” round number, reviewer verdicts so far, terminal status, what step was being executed\n- **Input that caused the failure**: if it's a file, the full path. If it's JSON input (e.g., raw LLM response), include verbatim or truncated with full version saved to a separate file in the run directory\n- **Expected format**: what the component expected to receive (schema name, example)\n- **Component**: which tool/layer produced the error (source_path)\n- **Provenance**: who was running, which LLM, which model, invocation UUID\n\n### Step 3: Governance Script Receives Bug Report\nThe governance layer (board orchestrator, or COO controller) receives the bug report and decides the next action.\n\n### Step 4: Auto-Fix Attempt\n- Call the builder (llm-tool-builder when complete, Codex agent as interim) with the bug report\n- Builder analyzes the error, proposes a fix, applies it\n- If fix applied: relaunch the component from the step where it stopped (not from scratch)\n\n### Step 5: Verify Fix\n- If relaunched component succeeds: continue execution\n- If same error persists or new error occurs: full stop\n\n### Step 6: Full Stop and Report\n- If auto-fix fails: produce a final error report for the CEO\n- Include: original bug report + fix attempt details + why it failed\n- Run exits as `blocked` with the error chain documented\n\nThis pattern applies to **every governed component** in ADF. Any unrecoverable error follows this escalation path.\n\n---\n\n## Three-Tier Post-Mortem\n\nThe system learns at three different scopes. Each tier has its own trigger, focus, and output. They are NOT nested supersets ג€” each has a distinct scope.\n\n### Tier 1: Run Post-Mortem (run-specific)\n\n- **Scope**: single run (the rounds within one attempt)\n- **Trigger**: after every round completes\n- **Focus**: what happened in this specific run\n- **Does**: captures KPI snapshot (time per round, reviewer verdicts, issue counts, convergence rate) and updates the component's rulebook if the learning engine found new rules\n- **Does NOT**: fix anything or perform self-healing. Run post-mortem is an observer, not an actor. It must be fast and cheap because it runs after every round.\n- **Output**: `run-postmortem.json` in the run directory\n\n### Tier 2: Cycle Post-Mortem (job-specific)\n\n- **Scope**: single job (all runs/attempts for one job)\n- **Trigger**: when the job reaches a terminal verdict (frozen, blocked, resume_required)\n- **Focus**: what happened across all attempts for this specific job\n- **Does**: produces a job summary covering all runs, performs deeper learning analysis (why did it take N runs? what patterns emerged?), and performs self-healing (if the tool itself had a gap, identifies it and feeds a fix request before resuming)\n- **Lightweight case**: if the job froze within budget on the first run, this is just summarizing the single run's findings into a job summary. Very lightweight execution.\n- **Heavy case**: if the job took 4 runs to freeze, this has real work ג€” analyzing WHY it took 4 runs, what broke, what was learned, what was fixed, and whether the fixes actually helped\n- **Output**: `cycle-postmortem.json` ג€” job summary + learning findings + self-improvement recommendations\n\n### Tier 3: Jobs Post-Mortem (component-specific)\n\n- **Scope**: all jobs for a component (across all time)\n- **Trigger**: every N cycles (e.g., 3) or manually triggered. **Runs in a background process** ג€” does not block work.\n- **Focus**: how is the component performing overall\n- **Does**: deepest analysis across all jobs ג€” component-level KPIs (average rounds to freeze, most common failure categories, rule effectiveness scores), generic issue patterns across all jobs, systemic blind spots, and deep self-improvement recommendations that affect the component's architecture or review process itself\n- **Output**: component KPI baseline + generic issue summary + systemic improvement plan\n- **Cleanup**: after analysis, deletes the raw run/round data for analyzed jobs to prevent bloating. The summary must include the deleted jobs' last commit IDs so there is an audit trail back to the git history if deep-dive is ever needed.\n\n**Key distinction:**\n- **Run** captures (specific to rounds within one attempt)\n- **Cycle** fixes (specific to one job across attempts)\n- **Jobs** improves systemically (specific to the component across all jobs)\n\n---\n\n## System-Wide Application\n\nEverything described above applies to every governed component in ADF, not just the agent-role-builder. Any component that uses LLMs and goes through review gets:\n\n- A rulebook in its directory (per LLM agent if multiple agents)\n- A review prompt in its directory (per LLM agent)\n- The learning engine between review and fix\n- Delta compliance maps from the implementer\n- Fix items maps with implementer accept/reject freedom\n- Structured reviewer feedback with fix decision maps\n- Three-tier post-mortem (run/cycle/jobs)\n- Permanent audit trail with defined commit/push points\n- Split-verdict handling\n- Budget exhaustion protocol (no swallowing of blocking issues)\n- Arbitration only on minor items, result reflects partial acceptance\n\nThe shared infrastructure (`shared/learning-engine/`, `shared/llm-invoker/`, `shared/telemetry/`) serves all components equally. The per-component artifacts (rulebook, review prompt, runs) live in each component's directory.\n\n---\n\n## Empirical Evidence: First Review Cycle (Step 2f, 2026-03-28)\n\nThe first application of this review process was a 3-round code review of the ADF shared infrastructure (34 files). Results:\n\n### Timing Baseline\n\n| Round | Duration | Tool Calls | Tokens | Scope |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 (cold start) | ~3.5 min | 52 | 95K | Full review of 34 files |\n| 2 (verify fixes) | ~3.5 min | 33 | 50K | Changed files + fix items map |\n| 3 (final sweep) | ~2.5 min | 25 | 64K | Full sweep on clean state |\n| **Total** | **~9.5 min** | **110** | **~210K** | 3 blocking + 5 major resolved |\n\n### Key Finding: Compliance Map Prevents Missed Fixes\n\nRound 2 found a major security issue (R2-1): the Gemini shell injection fix was applied to the canonical invoker but NOT to the copy in shared-imports.ts. A compliance map that listed all locations where each fix applies would have caught this before the reviewer did. This is now system rule SYS-001 in the rule-book-guide.\n\n### Convergence Pattern\n\n- Round 1: 3 blocking, 5 major, 7 minor, 5 suggestions (cold start)\n- Round 2: 9 fixes accepted, 1 rejection accepted, 1 new major (missed copy)\n- Round 3: all fixes verified, 3 rejections accepted, 0 violations on full sweep ג€” **APPROVED**\n\n### Cost Observation\n\nRound 1 (cold start) is the most expensive ג€” reviewer reads everything. Rounds 2-3 are cheaper because the fix items map tells the reviewer exactly what changed. The delta compliance approach is validated: incremental rounds cost ~50-65% of the initial round.\n\n### Evidence\n\n- Round 1: `docs/v0/reviews/step2f-implementation-review.md`\n- Round 2: `docs/v0/reviews/step2f-round2-review.json`\n- Round 3: `docs/v0/reviews/step2f-round3-review.json`\n\r\n\n succeeded in 275ms:\n# ADF Architecture\n\nStatus: locked decisions\nLast updated: 2026-03-27\n\n---\n\n## Technology Stack\n\n| Layer | Decision |\n|---|---|\n| **Controller runtime** | Node.js + TypeScript |\n| **Process orchestration** | `node:child_process` |\n| **Schemas / validation** | Zod (TS-native) + JSON Schema (external consumers) |\n| **Thread persistence** | JSON files initially, DB later if needed |\n| **Memory persistence** | PostgreSQL + pgvector (Brain MCP engine) |\n| **LLM calls** | CLI-based (Codex CLI primary, Claude CLI fallback). No SDK dependency ג€” uses existing CLI auth and token pools. |\n| **Shell** | Bash is the primary supported shell. ADF CLI launches from Bash. PowerShell only for Windows-specific leaf tasks. |\n| **Python** | Allowed for specialist tools, not the controller |\n\n### Shell Policy\n\n- **Bash** is the default shell for ADF operations, scripts, and CLI entry point\n- **PowerShell** is restricted to Windows-specific tasks where Bash cannot reach (e.g., Windows registry, COM objects, native Windows APIs)\n- ADF is OS-agnostic ג€” Bash provides the cross-platform baseline\n\n### LLM Model Configuration\n\nADF uses CLI-based LLM calls via `node:child_process`. No API SDKs. Codex is the primary provider (more tokens available), Claude is the fallback.\n\n| Role | Primary (Codex) | Reasoning | Fallback (Claude) | Effort |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **Classifier** | `gpt-5.3-codex-spark` | `medium` | `claude --model haiku` | `medium` |\n| **COO** | `gpt-5.4` | `xhigh` | `claude --model opus` | `max` |\n\n**Classifier**: intent classification only. Fast, cheap model. No action permissions (`--sandbox read-only`). No bypass.\n\n**COO**: executive reasoning, planning, memory operations. Strongest available model. Full permissions:\n- Codex: `--dangerously-bypass-approvals-and-sandbox`\n- Claude: `--dangerously-skip-permissions`\n\n### LLM Call Pattern\n\n```\nUser input ג†’ Controller\n  ג†’ Classifier call (Codex spark, fast)\n  ג†’ Route to workflow\n  ג†’ COO call (Codex gpt-5.4 or Claude opus, strong)\n  ג†’ Validate + commit state\n```\n\nBoth calls use `codex exec` / `claude --print` for non-interactive execution. Prompt passed as argument, response captured from stdout/file.\n\n---\n\n## Architecture Model\n\n### Core Principle\n\nAgent as a **stateless reducer** (12-factor-agents). The controller is a deterministic orchestrator ג€” not an LLM, not a drifting session. Each turn is a pure function: `next_event = agent(thread)`.\n\n### Turn Flow\n\n```\nUser input\n  ג†“\nController ingress\n  ג†“\nLoad current state\n  - COO role\n  - current contracts\n  - active summary\n  - open loops\n  - tool registry\n  - rule versions\n  ג†“\nIntent classification\n  - bounded classifier model call\n  ג†“\nSelect workflow\n  - direct COO response\n  - tool path\n  - specialist path\n  - clarification / pushback\n  ג†“\nRetrieve scoped context\n  - only what this turn needs\n  ג†“\nExecute selected path\n  - COO call and/or tool/specialist call\n  ג†“\nValidate outputs\n  - contracts\n  - required artifacts\n  - status correctness\n  - approval boundaries\n  ג†“\nCommit state\n  - summary\n  - open loops\n  - minutes/context\n  - audit/logs\n  - resume/session state\n  ג†“\nReturn response\n```\n\n### Key Components\n\n1. **Thread model** ג€” typed event list (Zod). Event types: `user_input`, `classifier_result`, `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `coo_response`, `error`, `human_request`, `state_commit`. The thread IS the state ג€” single source of truth per session.\n\n2. **Controller loop** ג€” deterministic `thread ג†’ next_event` reducer. TypeScript program, not a framework invocation. Every routing decision, gate, and error path is explicit code.\n\n3. **Context engineer** ג€” `thread_to_prompt()` function. Assembles thread events + PARA facts + auto-recalled memory + daily residue + owned prompts into optimized context per LLM call.\n\n4. **Intent classifier** ג€” small bounded LLM call per turn. Zod-validated structured output. Decides: direct COO response, tool path, specialist path, clarification, pushback.\n\n5. **COO agent** ג€” user-facing reasoning worker. Called as a function per turn with assembled context. Emits structured outputs. Does not maintain internal state.\n\n6. **Tool schema registry** ג€” tools defined as Zod types. Discovery via registry, not hardcoded lists. Each tool has a contract: input schema, output schema, side effects, approval requirements.\n\n### Operational Principles\n\n- Input first goes to the controller, never directly to the COO\n- Intent classification is model-assisted (script alone is not enough for natural language)\n- Controller remains deterministic even when invoking a classifier\n- Fresh model calls per turn (classifier, COO, specialists)\n- Continuity comes from externalized state (contracts, summaries, open loops, PARA), not from trusting a raw running session\n\n### Component Contract Rule\n\nEvery component must have: root folder, contract, source files, build artifacts, runtime artifacts, test surface, and audit surface.\n\n### Agent Role Governance Rule\n\n**Every LLM-powered agent must have a defined role and strict contract created through the agent-role-builder tool.** No exceptions ג€” this applies to all models regardless of capability level (including cheap/fast classifier models). The review board exists because:\n\n1. A single LLM never thinks about everything\n2. Different LLMs think differently and catch blind spots of other LLMs\n3. There are only a handful of core agents ג€” the high effort is justified to prevent drift\n\nRoles are created via governed multi-LLM review (Codex + Claude pairs), validated against schemas, and frozen only when no material pushback remains.\n\nSee [components-and-layers.md](components-and-layers.md) for the full component/layer taxonomy.\n\n### No Shortcuts Rule\n\n**No component is done until it has been through its own governance process and the evidence exists.** This means:\n\n- No manual seeds, no happy path assumptions, no deferred fixes\n- Every tool must eat its own dog food\n- Evidence-based development: if there is no artifact proving it works, it does not work\n- This applies to every tool, role, and component without exception\n- If something cannot be fully completed now, it probably never will be ג€” do it right the first time\n\n### Tool Integration Rule\n\n**llm-tool-builder must always call agent-role-builder** when creating a new tool. Every LLM tool gets a governed role. No tool exists without a role.\n\n**agent-role-builder creates its own role** as the first act of the system ג€” proving it works by eating its own dog food.\n\nBootstrap order:\n1. agent-role-builder creates its own role (proves it works)\n2. llm-tool-builder is fixed to always call agent-role-builder\n3. llm-tool-builder(update) attaches the role from step 1 to agent-role-builder\n4. Verify: both tools produced valid governed artifacts ג€” evidence exists\n\n### Review Process Rule\n\n**Every component that goes through governed review must have:**\n- A `rulebook.json` in its directory ג€” accumulated high-level rules from past failures\n- A `review-prompt.json` in its directory ג€” defines domain-specific review focus\n- If the component has multiple LLM agents, each gets its own subfolder with its own rulebook and review prompt\n\n**Review rounds follow a structured protocol:**\n- Implementer produces delta compliance map (full sweep on first and last round only)\n- Implementer produces fix items map with accept/reject freedom (rounds 2+)\n- Reviewer produces structured verdict (approved/conditional/reject) with conceptual groups, severity, redesign guidance\n- Reviewer responds to implementer rejections (accept_fix/reject_fix/accept_rejection/reject_rejection)\n- Learning engine extracts rules between review and fix ג€” updates the component's rulebook.json\n\n**Arbitration is minor-only.** No arbitration on blocking/major items. Result is `frozen_with_conditions`, invoker decides acceptance.\n\n**Budget exhaustion:** each reviewer picks 1 most important fix, implementer fixes those only, run exits as `resume_required`. No silent swallowing.\n\nSee [review-process-architecture.md](review-process-architecture.md) for full specification.\n\n### Learning Engine Rule\n\n**The learning engine (`shared/learning-engine/`) runs between every review and fix cycle.** It extracts generalizable rules from review feedback and updates the specific component's `rulebook.json`. Same generic engine, different domain prompt per component (loaded from `review-prompt.json`).\n\n### Three-Tier Post-Mortem Rule\n\nEvery governed component has three levels of post-mortem:\n- **Run post-mortem** (after every round): KPI snapshot + rulebook update. No self-healing.\n- **Cycle post-mortem** (on terminal verdict): job summary + deeper learning + self-healing. Lightweight if frozen in budget.\n- **Jobs post-mortem** (every N cycles, background): component-wide KPIs + systemic improvement. Deletes raw data, keeps commit IDs for audit.\n\n### Error Escalation Rule\n\n**When any governed component hits an unrecoverable error, it must stop immediately and produce a structured bug report.** No silent retries, no parse hacks. The governance layer receives the bug report and attempts auto-fix via builder (llm-tool-builder when complete, Codex agent interim). If fix succeeds, relaunch from the failed step. If fix fails or error persists, full stop with error chain documented.\n\nBug report must include: what failed, where (step/phase), execution context, input that caused failure (file path or verbatim JSON), expected format, component source_path, and provenance.\n\nSee [review-process-architecture.md](review-process-architecture.md) for full specification.\n\n### Telemetry Rule\n\n**Every operation that consumes resources must emit telemetry.** This includes:\n- LLM calls (tokens in/out, cost estimate, latency, model, primary vs fallback)\n- Memory engine operations (query latency, embedding time, op counts)\n- Tool executions (run time, board rounds, review budget consumed)\n- Turn lifecycle (total time, per-phase breakdown)\n\nTelemetry is:\n- **Async and atomic** ג€” zero latency to caller, fire-and-forget via MCP\n- **Stored in PostgreSQL** ג€” same DB as the memory engine, dedicated `telemetry` table\n- **Queryable by the COO** ג€” via `query_metrics` and `get_cost_summary` MCP tools\n- **Used for governance** ג€” COO warns CEO about costly operations, identifies bottlenecks, drives self-improvement\n\n### Governance Source of Truth\n\nThe **memory engine is the single source of truth** for all governance once it's live:\n\n| What | Storage | Trust Level |\n|---|---|---|\n| Decisions | `content_type: \"decision\"` | locked (immutable) |\n| Rules | `content_type: \"rule\"` | locked (immutable) |\n| Conventions | `content_type: \"convention\"` | reviewed or locked |\n| Settings/thresholds | `content_type: \"setting\"` | reviewed |\n| Telemetry | `telemetry` table | append-only |\n\nDocs in `docs/v0/` are scaffolding ג€” they get imported as locked database records. After import, the database is truth, docs are read-only references.\n\nThe controller loads locked rules from the memory engine at turn start and injects them into classifier + intelligence context. Rules are not markdown files ג€” they're database records with trust levels and enforcement.\n\n---\n\n## 6-Layer Memory Stack\n\n| Layer | Name | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|\n| **A** | Routing | MEMORY.md + AGENTS.md ג€” tiny bootstrap/index, not a fact warehouse |\n| **B** | Session Recovery | LCM (lossless-claw) ג€” scoped to COO conversation layer, not controller execution state |\n| **C** | Durable Knowledge | PARA (`projects/`, `areas/`, `resources/`, `archives/`) ג€” single source of truth for facts |\n| **D** | Daily Residue | `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` ג€” what happened today, short-horizon continuity |\n| **E** | Auto-Recall | Gigabrain ג€” pre-prompt recall/capture, feeds context engineer |\n| **F** | Cross-Session Graph | OpenStinger (optional) ג€” semantic + temporal recall across sessions |\n\n### Memory Design Principles\n\n1. File truth beats magic ג€” durable truth lives in files, not hidden config\n2. Tiny bootstrap ג€” MEMORY.md and AGENTS.md stay lean and focused\n3. Layer separation ג€” each layer has exactly one job\n4. Patch, don't replace ג€” managed insert blocks, not wholesale rewrites\n5. Optional upgrades ג€” OpenStinger is additive, not required\n6. Non-destructive ג€” backups before any modification\n7. Human review stays in loop ג€” config changes proposed, not blindly applied\n\n### Integration Point\n\nThe **context engineer** is where 12-factor-agents meets memory-stack.  succeeded in 436ms:
- **Scope**: single run (the rounds within one attempt)
- **Trigger**: after every round completes
- **Focus**: what happened in this specific run
- **Does**: captures KPI snapshot (time per round, reviewer verdicts, issue counts, convergence rate) and updates the component's rulebook if the learning engine found new rules
- **Does NOT**: fix anything or perform self-healing. Run post-mortem is an observer, not an actor. It must be fast and cheap because it runs after every round.
- **Output**: `run-postmortem.json` in the run directory

### Tier 2: Cycle Post-Mortem (job-specific)

- **Scope**: single job (all runs/attempts for one job)
- **Trigger**: when the job reaches a terminal verdict (frozen, blocked, resume_required)
- **Focus**: what happened across all attempts for this specific job
- **Does**: produces a job summary covering all runs, performs deeper learning analysis (why did it take N runs? what patterns emerged?), and performs self-healing (if the tool itself had a gap, identifies it and feeds a fix request before resuming)
- **Lightweight case**: if the job froze within budget on the first run, this is just summarizing the single run's findings into a job summary. Very lightweight execution.
- **Heavy case**: if the job took 4 runs to freeze, this has real work ג€” analyzing WHY it took 4 runs, what broke, what was learned, what was fixed, and whether the fixes actually helped
- **Output**: `cycle-postmortem.json` ג€” job summary + learning findings + self-improvement recommendations

### Tier 3: Jobs Post-Mortem (component-specific)

- **Scope**: all jobs for a component (across all time)
- **Trigger**: every N cycles (e.g., 3) or manually triggered. **Runs in a background process** ג€” does not block work.

        round-1/

```

**Commit timing:**
- **Commit**: at the end of every round (all round artifacts). Preserves evidence atomically per round.
- **Push**: at the end of every cycle (when job reaches terminal state). Rounds accumulate locally, push happens once when done.
- Exception: if a round takes unusually long (watchdog timeout), commit before the next round starts so evidence isn't lost if the process crashes.

 succeeded in 584ms:
- Implementer produces fix items map with accept/reject freedom (rounds 2+)
- Reviewer produces structured verdict (approved/conditional/reject) with conceptual groups, severity, redesign guidance
- Reviewer responds to implementer rejections (accept_fix/reject_fix/accept_rejection/reject_rejection)
- Learning engine extracts rules between review and fix ג€” updates the component's rulebook.json

**Arbitration is minor-only.** No arbitration on blocking/major items. Result is `frozen_with_conditions`, invoker decides acceptance.

**Budget exhaustion:** each reviewer picks 1 most important fix, implementer fixes those only, run exits as `resume_required`. No silent swallowing.

See [review-process-architecture.md](review-process-architecture.md) for full specification.

### Learning Engine Rule

**The learning engine (`shared/learning-engine/`) runs between every review and fix cycle.** It extracts generalizable rules from review feedback and updates the specific component's `rulebook.json`. Same generic engine, different domain prompt per component (loaded from `review-prompt.json`).

### Three-Tier Post-Mortem Rule

Every governed component has three levels of post-mortem:
- **Run post-mortem** (after every round): KPI snapshot + rulebook update. No self-healing.
- **Cycle post-mortem** (on terminal verdict): job summary + deeper learning + self-healing. Lightweight if frozen in budget.
- **Jobs post-mortem** (every N cycles, background): component-wide KPIs + systemic improvement. Deletes raw data, keeps commit IDs for audit.

 succeeded in 502ms:
- **Verdict**: `approved` (ready to freeze), `conditional` (minor fixes only), or `reject` (blocking/major issues)
- **Conceptual groups**: findings grouped by root cause, not flat lists. Each group has:
  - Severity: `blocking`, `major`, `minor`, or `suggestion`
  - Specific findings with source section references
  - Redesign guidance: what to do about it
- **Residual risks**: things that aren't blocking but need tracking
- **Strengths**: what's good (not just criticism)
- **Fix items decision map** (rounds 2+): for each item in the implementer's fix map:
  - `accept_fix`: the fix resolves the finding
  - `reject_fix`: the fix is insufficient (with reason why)
  - `accept_rejection`: the implementer was right to reject the finding
  - `reject_rejection`: the implementer's rejection is invalid (with reason why)

This creates a proper negotiation protocol between implementer and reviewer, not a one-way broadcast. The implementer has freedom to push back on findings that lack context or contradict other requirements, and the reviewer must explicitly respond to each pushback.

### Step 5: On review failure (reject verdict)
When a reviewer rejects:
1. Learning engine digests the feedback and updates the component's `rulebook.json`
2. A fresh implementer receives: current artifact + updated rulebook + actionable fix checklist + prior compliance map
3. The implementer first mechanically walks every rule in the rulebook against the artifact, including any newly learned rules from this round
4. The implementer fixes both:
   - the reviewer findings
   - any rule-compliance gaps revealed by that rule walk
5. The implementer produces both a new compliance map (delta) and a fix items map
6. The round proceeds to the next review cycle

### Step 6: On review pass (approved/conditional)
When all reviewers approve or go conditional:
- Conditional items are fixed (specific minor changes only)

---

## Split Verdict Handling

When one reviewer approves and another rejects:

1. Fix only the rejecting reviewer's findings
2. Re-run only the rejecting reviewer in the next round ג€” don't waste the approving reviewer's time
3. When the rejecting reviewer approves or goes conditional, run one final sanity check with the previously-approving reviewer to catch any regressions the fixes may have introduced
4. Both must approve/conditional before the artifact can freeze

This cuts review cycles significantly ג€” prior runs re-ran both reviewers every round even when one was satisfied.

---

## Arbitration (Minor Items Only)

Arbitration is strictly limited to prevent swallowing real issues:

**When arbitration is allowed:**
- Only on `minor` and `suggestion` severity items where reviewers disagree on whether something needs fixing
- The leader can decide "this is acceptable as-is" for these items only
- The decision is logged with full rationale

**When arbitration is NOT allowed:**
- Never on `blocking` or `major` severity items
- Never to override a `reject` verdict
- Never to force a freeze when material pushback remains

**Budget exhaustion protocol (final round):**
When the review budget is about to run out and blocking/major items remain:
- The artifact does NOT freeze. Blocking issues cannot be swallowed.
- Each reviewer picks their single most important remaining fix (ultimatum)
- The implementer fixes those specific items only
- The decision is logged with full audit trail: what was fixed, what was deferred, and why
- The run exits as `resume_required` with deferred items documented
- The cycle post-mortem (described below) analyzes why the budget wasn't sufficient

**Arbitration result:**
When arbitration is used, the result must reflect it as partial acceptance ג€” `frozen_with_conditions` (distinct from clean `frozen`). The result lists all deferred minor items. The invoking component (the caller that requested the role/review) can then:
- Accept the conditional result
- Request more rounds to achieve full acceptance

The invoker has authority, not the tool.

---

## All Contracts Are JSON

Every input and output in this architecture is strict JSON with defined schemas. No markdown for machine interfaces. Markdown is for human-readable documentation only.

import { z } from "zod";

/**
 * Fix Items Map ג€” produced by the implementer when fixing review feedback.
 * Shows for each reviewer finding whether it was accepted (with fix) or rejected (with reason).
 *
 * The reviewer then responds with a Fix Decision Map accepting or rejecting each item.
 */

export const FixItem = z.object({
  finding_id: z.string().optional(),
  finding_group_id: z.string().optional(),
  severity: z.enum(["blocking", "major", "minor", "suggestion"]).optional(),
  action: z.enum(["accepted", "rejected"]),
  summary: z.string().describe("What was changed (if accepted) or why it was rejected"),
  evidence_location: z.string().optional().describe("Where in the artifact the fix was applied"),
  rejection_reason: z.string().optional().describe("Why the finding was rejected (only if action=rejected)"),
}).superRefine((value, ctx) => {
  if (!value.finding_id && !value.finding_group_id) {
    ctx.addIssue({
      code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
      message: "Fix item must include finding_id or finding_group_id",
      path: ["finding_id"],
    });
  }
});
export type FixItem = z.infer<typeof FixItem>;

export const FixItemsMap = z.object({
  schema_version: z.literal("1.0"),
  component: z.string(),
  round: z.number(),
  items: z.array(FixItem),
  generated_at: z.string().datetime(),
});
export type FixItemsMap = z.infer<typeof FixItemsMap>;

/**
 * Fix Decision Map ג€” produced by the reviewer in response to the implementer's Fix Items Map.
 * Each finding gets a decision: accept the fix, reject the fix, accept the rejection, or reject the rejection.
 */

export const FixDecision = z.object({
  finding_id: z.string().optional(),
  finding_group_id: z.string().optional(),
  decision: z.enum(["accept_fix", "reject_fix", "accept_rejection", "reject_rejection"]),
  reason: z.string().describe("Why this decision was made"),
}).superRefine((value, ctx) => {
  if (!value.finding_id && !value.finding_group_id) {
    ctx.addIssue({
      code: z.ZodIssueCode.custom,
      message: "Fix decision must include finding_id or finding_group_id",
      path: ["finding_id"],
    });
  }
});
export type FixDecision = z.infer<typeof FixDecision>;

export const FixDecisionMap = z.object({
  schema_version: z.literal("1.0"),
  reviewer_id: z.string(),
  round: z.number(),
  decisions: z.array(FixDecision),
  generated_at: z.string().datetime(),
});
export type FixDecisionMap = z.infer<typeof FixDecisionMap>;

import { z } from "zod";

/**
 * Compliance Map ג€” produced by the implementer before submitting for review.
 * Maps each rulebook rule to evidence of compliance in the artifact.
 *
 * - Full scope: all rules checked (first and last round)
 * - Delta scope: only rules relevant to changed sections (middle rounds)
 */

export const ComplianceEntry = z.object({
  rule_id: z.string(),
  status: z.enum(["compliant", "non_compliant", "not_applicable"]),
  evidence_location: z.string().describe("Section, tag, or line where compliance is demonstrated"),
  evidence_summary: z.string().describe("Brief explanation of how the rule is satisfied"),
});
export type ComplianceEntry = z.infer<typeof ComplianceEntry>;

export const ComplianceMap = z.object({
  schema_version: z.literal("1.0"),
  component: z.string(),
  scope: z.enum(["full", "delta"]).describe("full = all rules checked, delta = only changed sections"),
  git_commit: z.string().optional().describe("Git commit hash of the artifact version being checked"),
  artifact_path: z.string().describe("Path to the artifact being checked"),
  round: z.number(),
  entries: z.array(ComplianceEntry),
  unchecked_rules: z.array(z.string()).default([]).describe("Rule IDs not checked (delta mode only ג€” rules for unchanged sections)"),
  generated_at: z.string().datetime(),
});
export type ComplianceMap = z.infer<typeof ComplianceMap>;
